title: Buttermilk fried chicken
subtitle: Brined overnight, double-dredged, fried golden.
excerpt: A whole chicken jointed into eight pieces, brined in buttermilk for a day, dredged twice in seasoned flour, and fried in a heavy pan to a deep mahogany crust. Edna Lewis's logic: cold buttermilk, hot fat, patience for the colour.
sourceNotes: The technique draws on Edna Lewis's In Pursuit of Flavour (1988), out of public domain but in the public canon, and the older buttermilk-brining tradition recorded in Southern Workman bulletins (1900s, public domain). Hannah Glasse's Art of Cookery (1747) describes the earliest fried-chicken technique in English.

recipe.yieldDescription: 8 pieces — 2 wings, 2 drumsticks, 2 thighs, 2 breast halves
recipe.makeAheadNotes: The chicken must brine in buttermilk for at least 12 hours and up to 24 for the right texture. The dredge mix can be made days ahead.

content.text: An overnight bath in buttermilk does two things to chicken. The lactic acid breaks the muscle fibre gently so the meat stays tender. The fat in the buttermilk coats the bird and the dredge sticks to it without slipping off in the pan. Both effects matter and both are time-dependent. Brine for at least 12 hours; 24 is better.
Dredge twice. First in flour, then a quick dip back into the buttermilk that came off the chicken, then in flour again. The second coat is what gives the craggy mahogany surface you want.
What you need

items.prepNote: or lard for the traditional version

content.text: A deep cast-iron skillet at least 28 cm wide and 8 cm deep, or a Dutch oven. An instant-read thermometer is non-optional; oil temperature is the whole game.
Method
Brine the chicken

Whisk {{buttermilk}} of buttermilk in a large bowl with {{sea-salt-fine}} of salt, {{paprika-smoked}} of smoked paprika, {{black-pepper}} of cracked black pepper, {{cayenne}} of cayenne, and {{garlic}} of smashed garlic. Add the chicken pieces, turn to coat, and press a sheet of cling film down so all the meat is submerged. Refrigerate for 12 to 24 hours.
Set up the dredge

Whisk {{plain-flour}} of flour, {{cornflour}} of cornflour, {{paprika-sweet}} of sweet paprika, {{english-mustard-powder}} of mustard powder, {{sea-salt-fine}} of salt, and {{black-pepper}} of pepper in a wide shallow bowl. The cornflour is what crisps the coating.
Double-dredge

Lift a piece of chicken from the buttermilk, letting most of it drip off. Lay in the flour, turn, press, and lift back into the buttermilk for a 5-second dip. Drop back into the flour and turn again, pressing the coating on firmly. Place on a wire rack. Repeat with all 8 pieces. Let stand for 15 minutes so the coating sets.
Heat the oil
Pour {{groundnut-oil}} of oil into the skillet to a depth of 5 cm. Heat to 175°C, using the thermometer to check. The oil should shimmer; a pinch of flour should bubble fiercely on contact.
Fry in batches

Slide in 4 pieces at a time, skin side down. The temperature will drop; adjust the heat to keep the oil between 160 and 165°C while the chicken cooks. Fry for 7 minutes a side, turning once carefully with tongs. The thigh and drumstick take a little longer than the breast and wing.

Chicken is done when the thermometer at the thickest part reads 75°C in the white meat, 80°C in the dark. Lift onto a rack set over a tray; don't drain on kitchen paper, which steams the underside and ruins the crust. Bring the oil back up to 175°C and repeat with the second batch. Rest the cooked pieces 10 minutes before serving so the juices settle.
Troubleshooting

items.fix: Use a thermometer. The oil should sit between 160 and 165°C while the chicken cooks. If it falls below 150°C, lift out a piece and let the oil recover.

items.symptom: Coating burnt before chicken cooked through
items.cause: Oil too hot at the start.
items.fix: Start at 175°C, drop the temperature once the chicken goes in. If the coating darkens fast, the oil is at 190°C; pull off the heat for 30 seconds.

items.symptom: Coating slipped off in the pan
items.cause: Chicken not given time to rest after dredging.
items.fix: Always rest the dredged chicken on a rack for 15 minutes before frying. The flour-buttermilk paste needs to dry slightly to stick.

items.cause: Drained on kitchen paper instead of a rack.
items.fix: Rest fried chicken on a wire rack set over a tray. Paper traps steam and softens the bottom crust.

items.symptom: Dark meat still raw at the bone
items.cause: Pieces too thick or fry time too short.
items.fix: Joint the chicken so all pieces are roughly the same thickness; trim the breast in half if needed. Use a thermometer: 80°C at the thigh bone is right.

content.text: Variations

Nashville hot adds 4 tablespoons of cayenne whisked into 200 ml of the hot frying oil, brushed over the cooked pieces while they rest. Korean-style cuts the chicken smaller, dredges twice, and dresses with a soy-honey-gochujang glaze after the second fry. For the classic Edna Lewis Virginia version, fry in lard or a 50/50 mix of lard and butter; the flavour is richer and the colour deeper.
Where this dish lives

Fried chicken belongs to the African-American Sunday tables of the South, brought from West African cooking traditions and developed over generations in Virginia, North Carolina, and Georgia kitchens. The pan-fried, lard-rich version Edna Lewis recorded in the Freetown of her childhood is the canonical one. The chain-restaurant version owes everything to those kitchens and the cooks who invented this technique long before it became national.
